content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 20, 2016

Vicki Nichols
117 Caledonia Street #C
Sausalito, CA 94965

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-16-209

Dear Ms. Nichols:

     This letter responds to your request for advice concerning your duties as a Sausalito
Planning Commissioner under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Would you have a conflict of interest in a decision affecting property within 300 feet of
your residence?

2. Would you have a conflict of interest based on a campaign contribution received from an
applicant if the contribution was received in 2012?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. You will not have a conflict of interest. A conflict of interest only exists if a decision
will have a material financial effect on a financial interest as specified in Section 87103. Since you
do not have a financial interest that could be affected by the decision, you would not have a conflict
of interest under the Act.

2. No. Campaign contributions only give rise to a conflict of interest for a Planning
Commissioner if the Commissioner has received a contribution of more than $250 within the
preceding 12 months from a party or from any participant. Both because of the size of the

___________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-209
Page No. 2

contribution in question ($250) and the fact that it was received more than 12 months ago, Section
84308 does not apply.

FACTS

You are a Planning Commissioner for the City of Sausalito. Recently the Planning
Commission considered a project at 101 Caledonia that was formerly the Cinemark Sausalito Movie
Theater.² The theater has been closed since January 2016 due to the Cinemark Company choosing
to not renew their lease. The building was subsequently vacated including removal of all equipment
and seating associated with the previous movie use.

The application for the movie theater is requesting exterior modifications with a change to
signage and a slight increase to the roof line. The proposal is to include two theaters with a
restaurant on the ground floor and the second floor to be used as offices. The staff report and public
notice describe the project as follows:

• Design Review Permit for exterior renovations which include modifications to the roof line;
• Conditional Use Permit for a theater, restaurant, and second level office;
• Sign Permit for business identification signage and theater marquee; and
• Encroachment Agreement for an awning and exterior building materials.

Your question concerns two potential sources of conflicts of interest.

1. You were a candidate for the 2012 City Council race and received campaign
contributions. One contributor is a property management firm that operates in the area in which you
live. You received a $250 contribution from the firm in 2012.

2. You are a renter near the project site and have lived on the northern end of the block for
over 30 years. You live at the end of the block on Caledonia between Pine and Turney Streets with
the proposed theater at the southwest end and your home at the northwest end of that block. You
have rented there for approximately 33 years on a month-to-month basis. All the tenants in the
building rent on a month-to-month basis.

Because the project is in line with your building there is no view impact. There may be an
increase in traffic with two new theaters being added to the Cinemark theater use. Much of the
parking is grandfathered from the Cinemark theater which was approved in about 1995. You are
concerned that the project has had a thorough review of needed parking requirements and that the
surrounding neighbors and community are able to park. As it is now residents require a paid parking
permit to park after 6:00 pm daily.

You noted that there is currently a large restaurant next to the theater with a small courtyard
that includes a dental office. The next building is a residential building with approximately five
rental units. The entire neighborhood is currently very developed with restaurants and other
businesses.

² You stated that the Planning Commission considered the project on October 5, 2016 but you abstained from
any participation in your official capacity.

File No. A-16-209
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a
conflict of interest may arise under the Act. Section 87103 includes the following:

"1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

"2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

"3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

"4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

"5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
$460 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

"6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the
personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family."

Of these interests, the only interest implicated by your facts is an interest in real property.
While an interest in real property includes a leasehold interest, Regulation 18233 provides that a
periodic tenancy of one month or less is excluded from the Act's definition of "interest in real
property" and "leasehold interest." Because you rent your residence on a month-to-month basis,
you do not have a financial interest in the property that could give rise to a conflict of interest under
the Act. Thus, under the facts presented, we conclude that you do not have a conflict of interest
under Section 87100 of the Act.

Campaign Contributions

You also asked about campaign contributions. Campaign contributions do not give rise to a
conflict of interest for elected officials under Section 87100. The Act expressly exempts campaign
contributions from the definitions of "income" and "gift." (Sections 82028 and 82030; See also
Dauber Advice Letter, No. A-15-151.)

File No. A-16-209
Page No. 4

In certain narrow situations, however, where an official is a member of an appointed board
or commission and is also running for office and receiving campaign contributions, campaign
contributions may give rise to a conflict of interest under Section 84308. Section 84308 requires an
officer of an agency who received a contribution of more than $250 within the preceding 12 months
from a party or from any participant shall disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding prior to
rendering any decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use
pending before the agency. Both because of the size of the contribution in question ($250) and the
fact that it was received more than 12 months ago, Section 84308 is not implicated in your
questions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

ktyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
